Thanks @varunkasyap 👍🏾
Use `skipIf(connection.vendor == "postgresql")` instead of mocking sqlite, otherwise the test will not run on other backends, e.g. Oracle. You'll probably need to do this on a dedicated test case since other test cases here skip unless PostgreSQL is the backend.

commits.author: Natalia
commits.message:
Discouraged automated AI reviews of pull requests.

Some contributors have been requesting Copilot reviews on pull requests
submitted to the Django repository. These automated reviews add noise to
the PR history, making it harder to follow the discussion, and Django
does not incorporate them into its review process.

This change discourages the practice by:

- Adding a `copilot-instructions.md` file instructing Copilot to decline
  review requests in this repository.
- Adding a checklist item to the PR template asking contributors not to
  request automated AI reviews for their PR.
- Documenting the policy in the contributing guidelines.

review_comments.author: charettes
review_comments.text:
I'm surprised `refresh_from_db` is necessary here as `supports_uuid7_function_shift` is only supported on Postgres and we use `RETURNING` on the insert for expressions (`create` delegates to `Model.save`). Are you sure this is the case?

We could be future proof with

```suggestion
        if connection.features.can_return_columns_from_insert:
            m.refresh_from_db()
```

but today there is no intersection with `supports_uuid7_function_shift and not can_return_columns_from_insert`.

review_comments.author: felixxm
review_comments.text: Sorry added by mistake :sweat_smile:

review_comments.author: charettes
review_comments.text:
Could we simply use a fixed `shift` instead?

As pointed out in the other PR Python-now and Database-now will risking shifting so we want to truly test things out here we should extract the database level `Now()` at insertion time and generate a UUIDv7 from it.

An alternative could we to do something like

```python
shift = timedelta(minutes=1)
m = UUIDModel.objects.create(uuid=UUID7(shift))
uuid_timestamp = datetime.fromtimestamp(
    self.uuid.time / 1000
)
self.assertGreaterEqual(datetime.now(timezone.utc) - uuid_timestamp, shift)
```

review_comments.author: felixxm
review_comments.text:
We shift `uuid_timestamp` by one minute to the future, so we should change the assertion to the:
```python
self.assertGreaterEqual(uuid_timestamp - datetime.now(timezone.utc), shift)
```
but even then I'm getting a difference of microseconds:
```
AssertionError: datetime.timedelta(seconds=59, microseconds=999227) not greater than or equal to datetime.timedelta(seconds=60)
```
so we would have to do something like this:
```python
shift = timedelta(minutes=1)
now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
m = UUIDModel.objects.create(uuid=UUID7(shift))
ts = int.from_bytes(m.uuid.bytes[:6])
uuid_timestamp = datetime.fromtimestamp(ts / 1000, tz=timezone.utc)
self.assertGreaterEqual(uuid_timestamp - now, shift)
```

review_comments.author: felixxm
review_comments.text: I can implement it this way, if preferred.

review_comments.author: jacobtylerwalls
review_comments.text:
Nice, I like getting rid of the `startswith()` call on fixed values.

I would also consider something like this:
```py
self.assertAlmostEqual(ts1.total_seconds(), ts2.total_seconds(), places=1)
```

... but I'm also fine with your last proposal @felixxm.

review_comments.author: felixxm
review_comments.text: Thanks, version with `total_seconds()` is less fragile.

comments.author: jacobtylerwalls
comments.text: Thanks for the review! If this looks right, I'll squash all but the last commit for the sneaked plural form from 11 months ago into a single commit.

review_comments.author: nessita
review_comments.text: I think this change should be reverted.

review_comments.author: nessita
review_comments.text:
If this was a manual remove, I would just leave these as they were, to avoid a repeated need to remove on each translation fetch. From chatGPT:
```
.po files follow the plural rules defined in the file header, specifically the Plural-Forms field. That header defines two things:

* nplurals: how many plural forms the language has.
* plural: the expression used to choose the index.

At runtime (Django / GNU gettext):
 
* The plural expression returns an index in the range 0..nplurals-1.
* Since nplurals=2, only index 0 or 1 will ever be selected.
* msgstr[2] will never be used even if it exists in the .po.

Effectively:

* msgstr[0] used for singular.
* msgstr[1] used for plural.
* msgstr[2] is unreachable and ignored.

The compiled .mo will behave as if only the first two forms exist.
```

review_comments.author: jacobtylerwalls
review_comments.text:
Seeing this skip here challenged me to reconsider what behavior we actually want to support here. (It's an unusual bug report in that it didn't come from a user: it came from a tangent during another code review.)

I think postgres is doing the right thing by complaining about mixed char and json types. (TIL that MySQL and SQLite are so loose with types!)

So, if we're going to build in some extra type-smoothing into `as_oracle()`, then we should only do it for a legitimate use case that also works on postgres.

I think a use case for mixing json and char would be for json-parsable-strings that are stored in a CharField. If you `Cast()` the json-parsable primitive, it should work, and does work on Postgres. But for Oracle you hit this condition where varchar and NCLOB are mixed, and so we end up needing the code change in `as_oracle()`.

I rejiggered the test case like this:
```diff
diff --git a/tests/db_functions/comparison/test_coalesce.py b/tests/db_functions/comparison/test_coalesce.py
index 776321b337..f0ec06cd8a 100644
--- a/tests/db_functions/comparison/test_coalesce.py
+++ b/tests/db_functions/comparison/test_coalesce.py
@@ -79,15 +79,12 @@ class CoalesceTests(TestCase):
                 qs = Author.objects.annotate(annotation=Coalesce(empty_query, 42))
                 self.assertEqual(qs.first().annotation, 42)
 
-    @skipUnless(
-        connection.vendor == "oracle",
-        "Oracle specific test for Coalesce with JSONField",
-    )
+    @skipUnlessDBFeature("supports_primitives_in_json_field")
     def test_json_coalesce_with_char_oracle_specific(self):
         """Test Coalesce with JSONField output_field on Oracle database."""
-        CoalesceJSONTestModel.objects.create(summary=None, metadata={"text": "test"})
+        CoalesceJSONTestModel.objects.create(summary="1", metadata={"text": "test"})
         # mixed JSON and CharField wrapped
         result = CoalesceJSONTestModel.objects.annotate(
-            coalesced_value=Coalesce("summary", "metadata", output_field=JSONField())
+            coalesced_value=Coalesce(Cast("summary", JSONField()), "metadata")
         ).first()
-        self.assertEqual(result.coalesced_value, {"text": "test"})
+        self.assertEqual(result.coalesced_value, 1)
```

Can you pursue this a little bit and see if I'm right about that? 🙏 

Feel free to adjust and use better values than "1" 😄

review_comments.author: sarahboyce
review_comments.text:
Hello hello 👋 
I couldn't fully investigate this as my local Oracle setup is not working (see https://forum.djangoproject.com/t/django-docker-box-oracle-ora-28001-the-account-has-expired-the-password-must-be-changed/44510)
I like this direction and I confirmed this is passing on postgreql. I wanted to:
- verify this test was failing before the changes and what error it failed with on Oracle
- decide whether I think the current version of the test should continue to fail on Oracle (as it does with postgresql)
- decide whether I think the test should belong in `tests/model_fields/test_jsonfield.py`

@kanin-kearpimy you might be able to add your opinions here as well.
(Note that the test name should be changed.)

review_comments.author: kanin-kearpimy
review_comments.text:
Hi, @sarahboyce @jacobtylerwalls,

Thank you for idea. I did verify. Your approach is confirmed. 

### Postgres mixed type for coalesce
Django use `template="(%(expressions)s)::%(db_type)s"` did convert any type to native postgres json (`jsonb`, `json`) which make input and output of Coalesce equal; JSONField in this case. So, It should work fine for whom casting type first.

### Why oracle fail
Django use `"JSON_QUERY(%(expressions)s, '$')"` if field if `JSONField`. `JSON_QUERY` type's outputs only include `VARCHAR2`, `CLOB`, `BLOB` ([reference](https://arc.net/l/quote/grnwybqr)). Such approach will throw error in oracle of unmatching of all Coalesce inputs / output.

### My next steps;
- We can revise the test to universally cover corss-database, mimicing @jacobtylerwalls. This should be in `tests/model_fields/test_jsonfield.py` instead.
- Revise this PR following Django convention (comments, function name, etc,.).

I update PR with my thought. Please let me know if you need some clarification.
